hub_subtitle: Business English para Finanças Corporativas, Entrevistas e Carreira Internacional

lesson.menu_title: Numbers Do Not Lie -- Discussing Financial Performance
lesson.menu_desc: Comentar performance com precisão: a linguagem de tendência e comparação -- 33 slides
lesson.subtitle: Lesson 13 -- Numbers Do Not Lie
lesson.title_tag: Professor View -- Felipe Pimenta | Lesson 13 -- Numbers Do Not Lie
lesson.grammar_point: the language of trends and comparison

listenings.text: I have covered listed companies for fifteen years, and I will tell you the mistake I see finance chiefs make again and again. They read me a number. Revenue was ninety million. And they stop, as if the number were the point. But a single number is a photograph, and I do not invest in photographs. I invest in the film. So the question I am really asking, every time, is not what the number is, it is which way it is moving and how fast. Is it up year on year, or only up because last year was a disaster? Is the growth like for like, or did you just buy it? Did the margin edge up a point, or surge, or quietly plateau while nobody was looking? Tell me the direction, the speed, and the comparison, and I can price the business. Tell me only the number, and all you have given me is a still frame of something that is already moving. The best finance people I deal with never present a figure naked. They dress it in a trend and a comparison, because they know that on its own a number means almost nothing at all.

The hardest discipline in reporting performance is telling the truth about a good quarter. Anyone can spin a bad one. The temptation with a good one is to let the headline do the talking and hope nobody strips it back. So here is the rule I hold myself to. Before I present a single figure to the board, I separate the underlying performance from the one-offs. If revenue jumped, I ask myself honestly: is that real momentum, or did a delayed deal simply land in this quarter instead of the last one? If the margin looks wonderful, I check whether a cost was genuinely lower or merely offset by something that will reverse next period. And then I present both numbers, the headline and the underlying, side by side, because the board that trusts you in the good quarter is the board that believes you in the bad one. Precision is not pessimism. Describing performance accurately, with the right comparison and the honest caveat, is the single fastest way a finance chief earns the right to be believed.

items.situation: "Revenue is ninety million. A director asks: is that good?"
tips: It is up eleven per cent year on year, and on a like-for-like basis closer to five.
Never present a number naked. Give the direction and the comparison.

items.situation: "The margin moved from twenty-one to twenty-two per cent. Describe it."
tips: Gross margin edged up a point, from twenty-one to twenty-two, driven by mix.
Small move? Say 'edged up'. Keep the verb honest to the size of the change.

items.situation: "New sign-ups doubled in a month. Describe the trend, not just the number."
tips: Sign-ups surged after the launch, and the momentum has held into this quarter.
Big, fast move? 'Surged'. Then say whether the momentum lasted.

items.situation: "A one-off tax gain flattered the profit. The board looks pleased."
tips: I want to flag that the beat is a one-off; underlying profit was broadly flat.
Separate the headline from the underlying. Volunteer the one-off before anyone asks.

items.situation: "Growth was strong for three quarters and then stopped. Describe it."
tips: Growth plateaued in the fourth quarter after outperforming plan in the first three.
Name the shape of the trend, not only the last data point.

items.situation: "Costs rose, but so did volumes. An analyst asks about profit."
tips: Higher costs were largely offset by higher volumes, so profit was broadly flat.
When two forces cancel out, say 'offset' and give the net result.
